documents.title: Bank Statement Red Flags
documents.body:
# Bank Statement Red Flags

## Signal hierarchy

Analysts should separate isolated operational noise from repeated deterioration:

- A single NSF fee can be structured around when DSCR remains above 1.25x and liquidity is otherwise healthy.
- Two or more NSF or returned payment events within 90 days are a strong manual-review signal.
- Five or more negative balance days usually indicate a decline unless a verified timing explanation is documented.

## Liquidity stress patterns

The following statement patterns are more concerning than headline revenue alone:

- Marketing, inventory, or contractor debits posting materially ahead of collections.
- Tax or payroll debits that are delayed, fragmented, or replaced with partial payments.
- Frequent same-day sweeps that leave the operating account near zero before customer cash lands.

## How analysts should respond

Use structure when the business is otherwise healthy:

- Add a reserve when the file has one isolated NSF event or a shallow minimum balance dip.
- Shorten reporting cadence when balances repeatedly approach zero but do not turn negative.
- Escalate to manual review when statement stress appears alongside customer concentration or rising chargebacks.

documents.title: Covenant Monitoring Guide
documents.body:
# Covenant Monitoring Guide

## Standard post-close triggers

The following thresholds should move a live account onto the watchlist:

- DSCR below 1.15x.
- Two consecutive months of negative net cash flow.
- Overdue receivables above 18% of the current book.
- Top customer concentration above 40%.
- Two or more returned payments or NSF events in any rolling 90-day window.

## Structural responses

When a watchlist trigger trips, respond with the lightest structure that still protects downside:

- Increase reserve by 3% to 5% before cutting the facility if collections remain orderly.
- Tighten borrowing-base eligibility if a single obligor is driving the deterioration.
- Require weekly reporting when liquidity is thinning faster than receivables are turning.

## Escalation threshold

Escalate from watchlist to remediation when multiple triggers stack at once. For example:

- DSCR below 1.00x plus repeated negative balance days.
- Rising overdue receivables plus shrinking liquidity runway.

documents.title: Fraud And KYB Overrides
documents.body:
# Fraud And KYB Overrides

## When fraud signals intersect with underwriting

Some files look financeable on gross cash flow but require an underwriting override because the operating pattern points to fraud or control gaps:

- Processor concentration plus rising chargebacks can signal channel abuse or poor fulfillment.
- Sudden refund spikes without matching revenue growth can mean hidden product issues.
- Ownership or bank-account mismatches discovered during KYB should block approval until resolved.

## Practical treatment

For borderline files, combine fraud signals with credit signals instead of treating them separately:

- One weak signal may justify only a reserve or shorter tenor.
- Several weak signals together should push the file into manual review even when DSCR is technically above 1.00x.

## Operating note

E-commerce borrowers deserve tighter interpretation because ad spend can temporarily mask weak unit economics. In that segment, rising chargebacks and declining liquidity should be treated as a linked problem rather than separate isolated events.

documents.title: Invoice Advance Policy
documents.body:
# Invoice Advance Policy

## Eligible receivables

Receivables can support an invoice advance only when the work is complete and the paper is collectible:

- Invoice due dates should be 60 days or less from issue.
- The obligor must be identifiable and creditworthy.
- No known offsets, disputes, or contra balances should exist.
- Overdue invoices are not eligible for the base borrowing formula.

## Advance-rate bands

Advance rates should tighten as obligor risk rises:

- 80% to 85% advance rates are reserved for low-dilution books with overdue receivables below 5% and obligor concentration below 30%.
- 70% to 75% advance rates fit concentrated but still collectible books where top customer concentration is between 30% and 45%.
- Advance rates below 70% or outright ineligibility are appropriate when aging exceeds 15%, dilution is elevated, or documentation is incomplete.

## Ongoing monitoring

Invoice lines require more than an origination decision:

- Recalculate eligibility weekly when the borrower is growing quickly.
- Freeze new advances when aging breaches 18% or when the largest obligor misses a payment date.
- Step down the advance rate before moving to a full block whenever the deterioration is still reversible.

documents.title: Merchant Underwriting Reserve Policy
documents.body:
# Merchant Underwriting Reserve Policy

## Core approval criteria

Cash flow files can be approved without structural enhancement when all of the following are true:

- Proxy DSCR is at or above 1.35x.
- Liquidity covers at least 3 months of fixed operating costs.
- Top customer concentration stays at or below 35% of recent invoice volume.
- Overdue receivables stay below 10% of the receivables book.
- No more than one minor statement exception appears in the trailing 90 days.

## Reserve triggers

A reserve recommendation of 5% to 10% is appropriate in merchant underwriting when the business still covers debt service but one or more structural concerns are present:

- Top customer concentration rises above 35%.
- One NSF or returned ACH fee appears in the last 90 days.
- Chargebacks or dispute losses rise above 0.6% of gross sales.
- The minimum observed cash balance falls below one month of fixed costs.
- Revenue remains solid but invoice aging drifts into the 10% to 15% overdue band.

## Manual review triggers

Move the file to manual review when the company may still be financeable, but a policy override or tighter monitoring is required:

- DSCR falls between 1.00x and 1.20x.
- Two or more negative balance days appear in the trailing 90 days.
- Revenue declines more than 15% month over month without a signed pipeline explanation.
- Overdue receivables rise into the 15% to 20% band.

## Decline triggers

Decline the request when repayment capacity or control quality breaks below the minimum tolerance:

- DSCR is below 1.00x.
- Five or more negative balance days appear in the trailing 90 days.
- Repeated NSF events show weak payment discipline.
- Chargebacks exceed 1.2% of gross sales.
- Unresolved payroll or tax delinquencies are visible in statements or diligence.

documents.title: Revenue Quality Framework
documents.body:
# Revenue Quality Framework

## Recurring revenue

The cleanest cash flow borrowers usually show repeat invoicing or repeat processor payouts from the same customer set:

- Recurring revenue above 60% supports higher confidence in forward collections.
- Recurring revenue below 40% requires more caution because the next quarter may not resemble the last quarter.

## Concentration

Concentration drives both repayment risk and structure:

- Top customer concentration below 30% is strong.
- Concentration between 30% and 40% is acceptable with monitoring.
- Concentration above 40% should usually trigger reserve, tighter covenants, or a lower advance rate.

## Volatility and leakage

Revenue quality weakens when the gross sales line is offset by leakage:

- Monthly inflow volatility above 25% should reduce confidence in straight-line forecasting.
- Refunds and chargebacks above 1% of gross sales should move the file into heightened review.
- Fast growth is not enough on its own if cash conversion is getting worse.

documents.title: Risk Committee Brief Q1 2026
documents.body:
# Risk Committee Brief Q1 2026

## Service businesses

The committee remains comfortable approving service businesses with an isolated payment exception when the broader file is clean:

- One NSF event should not force a decline when DSCR remains above 1.30x, revenue is recurring, and customer concentration stays below 40%.
- In those cases, a modest reserve or weekly cash reporting is usually enough.

## Distribution and invoice finance

For distribution and invoice-backed products, the committee prefers disciplined structure over oversized limits:

- Keep full requested size only when eligible receivables support it at the target advance rate.
- Reduce facility size rather than stretching eligibility assumptions.

## E-commerce

The committee is materially more conservative on e-commerce and marketplace-heavy borrowers:

- Rising chargebacks plus thinning liquidity runway should push the file toward manual review or decline.
- Paid media intensity is not itself a problem, but it becomes one when cash conversion weakens.
